author: role.analyst.asymmetric-multiscreen

dimensionSummaries.dimension.player-fantasy: The player or group commands four coordinated heroes as a small force, alternating solidarity, formation tactics, independent action, and friendly competition.
dimensionSummaries.dimension.core-loop: Enter a course, explore public and private spaces, assign items or formations, solve multi-body obstacles, collect enough Force to empower the shared sword, defeat the course threat, break the final barrier, and receive a performance ranking.
dimensionSummaries.dimension.verbs: Top-down sword and item actions combine with group, split, leader-swap, carry, and four formations; multiplayer gives individuals direct control while solo play treats the four as a reconfigurable unit.
dimensionSummaries.dimension.world-state: Course-local enemies, switches, interiors, player positions, held items, collected Force, and the final barrier are synchronized; completion advances through eight regions of three courses each.
dimensionSummaries.dimension.topology: Courses alternate shared television spaces with interiors shown privately on connected handhelds, allowing hidden subspaces, simultaneous routes, and reunions without losing the public macro-scene.
dimensionSummaries.dimension.gating-revisit: Headcount, color, formation, item, public-private position, Force threshold, and course completion gate progress; insufficient Force can send the team back through the current course to search.
dimensionSummaries.dimension.puzzle-information: Puzzles communicate required formation, number, color, screen layer, direction, or item through object shape and response; group reconfiguration is frequently the answer grammar.
dimensionSummaries.dimension.dungeon-structure: The main adventure uses discrete courses rather than a continuous overworld, each with an internal arc of exploration, puzzles, mass combat, collection, and an end barrier or boss.
dimensionSummaries.dimension.exploration-reward: Force gems appear from enemies, vegetation, mechanisms, hidden spaces, and cooperative actions, making thorough route coverage both a score opportunity and a clearance requirement.
dimensionSummaries.dimension.growth-economy: Force is a course-local empowerment and gate resource, while item ownership is largely situational; stage results compare individual contribution rather than building a conventional persistent wallet economy.
dimensionSummaries.dimension.combat-boss: Large enemy groups make formation shape meaningful, while bosses and barriers test coordinated force, item use, and the ability to regroup after independent movement.
dimensionSummaries.dimension.npc-quest: Authored NPC interaction is secondary to human coordination; multiplayer participants help or compete, while solo leader switching and formation control substitute a command relationship for peer negotiation.
dimensionSummaries.dimension.narrative-performance: Episodic course framing, massed hero motion, transitions between public and private screens, barrier destruction, and end-course rankings deliver the primary theatrical rhythm.
dimensionSummaries.dimension.camera-blocking: A shared television camera frames public action, personal handheld screens frame interiors or hidden activity, and formation silhouettes keep the four-body unit readable during crowded scenes.
dimensionSummaries.dimension.teaching-feedback: Formation icons, immediate block and enemy reactions, visible Force accumulation, screen transfer, regroup commands, and course-end scoring teach through repeated local consequences.
dimensionSummaries.dimension.ui-input: Solo play can use a GameCube controller, while one-to-four-player modes use linked Game Boy Advance systems; leader, formation, item, Force, and screen location require clear mode indicators.
dimensionSummaries.dimension.recovery-accessibility: Course segmentation and local regrouping limit replay distance after mistakes, and solo support avoids mandatory partners; multiplayer still requires specialized handhelds and cables.
dimensionSummaries.dimension.perspective-implementation: Top-down 2D supports precise formation footprints and dense enemy fields, while the split public-private display model turns screen ownership into a spatial rule.
dimensionSummaries.dimension.hardware-multiplayer: One-to-four players are supported; handhelds plus GameCube link cables provide private views, while solo play can command all four heroes with formations from a standard controller.
dimensionSummaries.dimension.production-runtime: A faithful abstraction needs synchronized multi-display world state, public-private visibility rules, four-agent formation and regroup logic, course-local resource accounting, scalable player ownership, and stage-result scoring.

observationThemes.context: A solo player controls all four heroes in a course containing different obstacle and enemy geometries.
preconditions: Several formations and the split command are available.
playerInput: Choose a formation, move or attack as a unit, then split or change leader when precision is needed.
before: The current group footprint is poorly matched to the obstacle or threat.
action: Reconfigure the same four actors into a wide, long, cross, box, parade, or separated arrangement.
after: Their combined collision, reach, attack coverage, or object-pushing relation changes.
feedback: Formation icon, immediate rearrangement, synchronized animation, object movement, and enemy response expose the new capability.
consequences: Party composition remains constant while spatial configuration becomes the primary tactical verb.
observationThemes.factualDescription: Solo play lets one player command four heroes using several named formations, change the leader, gather the group, or separate an individual.
observationThemes.interpretation: Multiple bodies can act like a single tool whose function changes through geometry rather than inventory replacement.

observationThemes.context: A handheld-controlled hero enters a house, cave, or hidden subspace while other heroes remain in the television world.
preconditions: A connected handheld display is active.
The entrance transitions that hero into a private-view area.
playerInput: Enter the subspace, manipulate its local objects, and return or affect the public area.
before: The public group cannot see the interior details and a cross-layer dependency is unresolved.
action: Operate privately while the synchronized course continues on the shared display.
after: An item, actor, projectile, switch, or route can cross or affect the boundary.
feedback: The hero disappears from the television, appears on the handheld, and visible or audible consequences propagate between screens.
consequences: Information asymmetry and simultaneous action become level-design materials rather than menu separation.
observationThemes.factualDescription: Connected handhelds show interiors and other private spaces when a hero leaves the shared television area, while both displays remain part of one synchronized course.
observationThemes.interpretation: A visibility boundary is most meaningful when actions can cross it and other participants must infer or communicate private state.

observationThemes.context: The team reaches a course-ending dark barrier after passing through optional and mandatory Force sources.
preconditions: The shared sword has accumulated some Force.
The barrier requires a visible course-local threshold.
playerInput: Attempt the barrier, then if necessary revisit the course and extract missed Force from combat, objects, or mechanisms.
before: The route endpoint is reached but collection may be insufficient.
action: Convert thorough interaction and exploration into a shared empowerment state.
after: At the threshold the barrier can be broken; below it, the route remains closed and the search continues.
feedback: Collect display, sword power indication, gem pickups, barrier reaction, and return warp make the requirement explicit.
consequences: Optional-looking side interactions become part of macro progression without requiring every exact collectible.
observationThemes.factualDescription: Each course ends at a dark barrier that can only be destroyed after enough Force has been gathered from enemies, scenery, mechanisms, and other course events.
observationThemes.interpretation: A forgiving aggregate threshold can reward broad engagement while letting players choose which opportunities satisfy it.

observationThemes.context: Multiple participants complete a cooperative course after helping, separating, and competing for resources.
preconditions: Individual actions have generated trackable contribution signals.
playerInput: Finish the course and review the comparative result.
before: The group has a shared success but ambiguous individual standing.
action: Transform accumulated play traces into a staged ranking.
after: Each participant receives an explicit comparative outcome without undoing collective clearance.
feedback: End-stage presentation, category results, placement, and peer reaction expose how the session is being interpreted.
consequences: Competition is deferred into a social epilogue, allowing cooperative dependency to dominate the course itself.
observationThemes.factualDescription: After a stage, multiplayer performance is evaluated and participants are ranked from several tracked aspects of play.
observationThemes.interpretation: Post-stage comparison can preserve playful rivalry while preventing constant scoring from obscuring shared spatial goals.

observationThemes.context: A dense enemy group surrounds or channels the four-hero unit.
preconditions: The group can change formation without losing course progress.
Enemy arrangement creates a readable directional threat.
playerInput: Select a formation whose footprint and attack coverage match the threat, then regroup after disruption.
before: A compact or misaligned formation exposes the unit to damage or limits coverage.
action: Change geometry and attack through the corresponding lanes.
after: More enemies are covered, a narrow route is respected, or the group avoids surrounding attacks.
feedback: Distinct silhouette, synchronized attack arcs, hit effects, health response, and one-button regroup show whether the choice worked.
consequences: Crowd difficulty can grow through arrangement while recovery remains a clear reconfiguration action.
observationThemes.factualDescription: The game fields large enemy groups and gives formations different directional coverage and spatial uses, with a direct gather command after separation.
observationThemes.interpretation: When formation shape is visually obvious, encounter density can test configuration instead of demanding more individual commands.

contributionHypotheses: A party can become a reconfigurable spatial tool when formation changes alter footprint, force, and coverage under one input grammar.
Public and private synchronized views can turn visibility boundaries into cooperative level mechanics.
A course-wide aggregate collection threshold may reward optional engagement while preserving route flexibility.
uniqueOrNonportable: The exact four-hero mythology, named sword and Force resource, characters, regions, story incidents, formations' branded names, and course sequence are recognizable authored surface content and must not be reused.
Specific room layouts, screen-transition scenes, bosses, ranking categories, item roster, and audiovisual identity are nonportable.
The GameCube-to-GBA cable implementation is a historical platform expression; only abstract public-private synchronized views may be compared.
openQuestions: A primary-play trace should distinguish which public-private effects are mandatory in solo controller play versus handheld play.
Exact course recovery, individual scoring weights, and Force retention rules need formal state traces before pattern tests encode them.
